summary: Joel Spolsky introduces his monthly book recommendation as a springboard for a longer essay about the cultural divide in software development. He frames modern programming as split between two major ideological camps: Unix culture and Windows culture. The post itself is essentially a teaser directing readers to his full essay on 'Biculturalism' in software development.
key_points: Modern software development is divided into two dominant ideological cultures: Unix and Windows
The cultural divide between these camps is significant enough to warrant a full treatise
Spolsky uses his Book of the Month feature as a vehicle for deeper technical and cultural commentary
money_quotes: Modern software development is a world mostly divided into two large ideological cultures: The Culture of Unix Programming and The Culture of Windows Programming.
key_insight: The software industry is fundamentally split between Unix and Windows programming cultures, each with its own ideology and worldview.
